messages.content: Should states ban foreign acquisition of farmland to protect food security, or welcome it as investment and technology transfer?
Should countries require companies to disclose pay ratios by gender and ethnicity publicly, or keep such data internal?
Should governments allow “employee ownership trusts” to receive tax breaks when founders sell businesses to workers?
Should public procurement prioritize domestic suppliers even when they are more expensive, for resilience and jobs?
Should governments enforce maximum commuting times by requiring housing near job centers, or is that social engineering?
Should couples be socially expected to split household labor 50/50 regardless of income differences, or is specialization reasonable?
Should schools teach students to practice mindfulness and emotional regulation as core curriculum, or is that beyond education’s remit?
Should employers be allowed to require always-on cameras during remote work meetings, or is that a workplace privacy violation?
Should parents limit children’s participation in competitive sports due to injury and burnout risks, or is competition developmentally valuable?
Should society view adult children who go “no contact” with parents as justified self-protection or as a troubling cultural trend?
Should governments set legal standards for what counts as “misleading political claims” in campaign ads, enforced by an independent body?
Should countries allow political parties to use AI to generate personalized campaign messages for individual voters, as long as it’s disclosed?
Should parliamentary systems limit the power of party whips to allow more conscience voting, or does that break governance?
Should countries adopt recall referendums for judges, or does that end judicial independence?
Should the EU expand qualified-majority voting to more domestic-policy areas like taxation, or is unanimity essential for legitimacy?
Should countries with separatist movements (like Spain, Indonesia, or Nigeria) criminalize pro-independence advocacy, or protect it as political speech?
Should governments require public officials to disclose algorithms used for welfare, policing, and immigration decisions?
Should states allow “predictive child welfare” analytics to flag families at risk, or is that punitive surveillance of poverty?
Should courts treat “digital self-defense” (doxxing an alleged wrongdoer) as a serious offense, or as sometimes justified accountability?
Should prisons allow incarcerated parents regular extended contact visits to maintain family bonds, even if it complicates security?
Should countries abolish cash entirely within a decade to reduce crime and tax evasion, or keep cash as a freedom-preserving public option?
Should governments require “kill switches” or remote disablement capabilities in all consumer drones?
Should nations allow private citizens to own and operate small satellites, or restrict space activity to licensed operators only?
Should AI companies be required to compensate people whose voices are used to train speech models, even when sourced from public media?
Should governments ban virtual influencers aimed at children, or treat them like any other advertising character?
Should countries impose strict liability on companies for data breaches, even when they followed industry best practices?
Should schools in multicultural societies allow opt-outs from certain lessons (sex ed, evolution, religion), or enforce a single shared curriculum?
Should governments restrict religious calls to prayer over loudspeakers in dense urban areas, or protect them like other community noise?
Should states ban “ethnic profiling” in policing entirely, or allow narrowly defined use based on suspect descriptions?
Should countries legalize police unions’ ability to bargain over discipline procedures, or exclude discipline from collective bargaining?
